rationale: The cited Bayus, Jain, and Rao (2001) paper is used repeatedly in core framing, not just as a passing background reference: the authors rely on it for key claims about preannouncement prevalence, competitive preemption, and vaporware-related signaling and penalties. It also informs construct development (e.g., time-to-introduction as a signal) and the empirical context of software/hardware preannouncements. In the discussion, the citing paper explicitly positions its contribution relative to Bayus et al. by arguing that even if vaporware can deter entry, unreliable preannouncement behavior can reduce future shareholder-value gains. This is a meaningful qualification of prior implications rather than a mere mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Bayus et al. (2001) as a central prior in preannouncement/vaporware research and then qualifies its implications by showing that shareholder value depends on credibility conditions (specificity, reliability, and updating). It refines the prior strategic-vaporware view by adding a longer-run financial-cost boundary condition.
